rules.title: Heads-Up Poker Rules
rules.intro: Heads-up poker is a game of Texas Hold'em between exactly two players. It is the most skill-intensive format — every decision matters, and there is no one else at the table to shift the action. Here are the complete rules.
rules.goalDesc: Win all of your opponent's chips. Each hand, players compete for the pot — the sum of all bets made during that hand. You win the pot either by having the best five-card hand at showdown or by forcing your opponent to fold.
setupList: A standard 52-card deck is used, shuffled before each hand.
Each player starts with an equal chip stack (in Poker God — 1000 chips).
One player posts the small blind, the other posts the big blind.
Each player is dealt two private cards (hole cards) face-down.
rules.blindsDesc: Blinds are forced bets that ensure there is always something to play for. The small blind (SB) is half the big blind (BB). In heads-up, the dealer posts the small blind and acts first preflop, but acts last on all subsequent streets. In Poker God, the small blind is 10 and the big blind is 20.
rules.preflopDesc: Each player receives two hole cards. The small blind (dealer) acts first. They can fold, call (match the big blind), or raise. The big blind then responds. Betting continues until both players have put in equal amounts or one folds.
rules.flopDesc: Three community cards are dealt face-up in the center of the table. A new round of betting begins. The player who is not the dealer acts first from this point on.
rules.turnDesc: A fourth community card is dealt face-up. Another round of betting follows, with the same order as the flop.
rules.riverDesc: A fifth and final community card is dealt face-up. The last round of betting takes place.
rules.showdownDesc: If both players are still in the hand after the river betting, they reveal their hole cards. Each player makes the best five-card hand using any combination of their two hole cards and the five community cards. The player with the stronger hand wins the pot. If both hands are equal, the pot is split.
actionsList: Fold — give up your hand and forfeit the pot.
Check — pass the action without betting (only if no one has bet in the current round).
Call — match the current bet to stay in the hand.
Raise — increase the bet, forcing the opponent to call, re-raise, or fold.
All-in — bet all of your remaining chips.
headsUpList: There are only two players, so you play every hand — no sitting out.
Preflop, the dealer (small blind) acts first; postflop, the dealer acts last.
Aggression is critical — passive play is easily exploited one-on-one.
Hand ranges are much wider than at a full table. Hands like K-5 or Q-8 are often playable.
Position advantage is amplified — acting last gives you more information.
Reading your opponent and adjusting your strategy is the key skill.
rules.winDesc: At showdown, each player's best five-card hand is compared. Hand rankings from highest to lowest: Royal Flush, Straight Flush, Four of a Kind, Full House, Flush, Straight, Three of a Kind, Two Pair, One Pair, High Card. If both players have the same hand rank, the highest kicker (side card) decides the winner. If all five cards are identical in rank, the pot is split.

hands.title: Heads-Up Starting Hands
hands.intro: This chart shows the relative strength of starting hands in heads-up Texas Hold'em. Hands are rated from tier 1 (premium) to tier 5 (weakest). In heads-up, you should play a much wider range than at a full table.
hands.suitedNote: Upper-right triangle = suited (s), lower-left = offsuit (o), diagonal = pocket pairs.

about.title: About Poker God
about.description: Poker God is a free training platform for heads-up Texas Hold'em. It combines a reference library, decision trainers, and a full game with post-game analysis. Here is everything the platform offers.
about.botDesc: Play full Texas Hold'em hands against an AI bot. It evaluates hand strength, adjusts aggression, bluffing, and bet sizing according to the selected difficulty.
botFeatures: 10 difficulty levels from Beginner (loose, passive) to Professional (tight, aggressive, well-sized bets)
Full betting rules: minimum raise enforcement, all-in mechanics
Realistic card flip animations with stage-by-stage dealing
about.analysisDesc: After every hand, a side panel lets you replay each stage with arrow keys. Two viewing modes:
about.modeOpenDesc: Bot cards shown at every stage, even after a fold. Exact equity per street. Clean outs listed — cards that guarantee a win regardless of the remaining board.
about.modeClosedDesc: Bot cards hidden, simulating a real game. Blind equity calculated against all possible opponent hands. Conditional outs shown — cards with >60% win rate across opponent hand scenarios.
about.equityDesc: Equity is calculated by exhaustive enumeration (turn/river/flop) or Monte Carlo simulation (preflop). Outs are displayed on the flop and turn in both modes.
about.calcDesc: Pick any two starting hands from a visual card grid and instantly calculate their win probability using Monte Carlo simulation (15,000 samples). Results shown as percentages with a color equity bar.
about.callTitle: To call or not to call
about.callDesc: A scenario trainer for postflop decisions. You face a bet on the flop, turn, or river. After choosing call or fold, you see: your equity vs pot odds, whether the call is +EV, and a practical analysis covering implied odds, opponent tendencies, and position. 10 difficulty levels from obvious to razor-thin margins with non-round bet sizes.
about.threebetDesc: You opened with a raise and the opponent 3-bets. Should you call or fold? Equity is calculated against a realistic 3-bet range (not random hands). 10 difficulty levels with varying 3-bet sizing.
about.preflopDesc: Opponent opens preflop — decide between 3-bet, call, or fold. Three position modes: Out of Position (tighter thresholds), In Position (wider ranges), or Mixed. Equity calculated against the opener's range. Position-aware practical advice for each decision.
about.rulesDesc: Complete guide to heads-up Texas Hold'em: objective, setup, blinds, all five stages, player actions, heads-up strategy, and winner determination with kicker rules.
about.combosDesc: All ten poker hand rankings from High Card to Royal Flush with examples.
about.handsDesc: 13×13 grid of all 169 starting hands, color-coded in five tiers for heads-up. Each hand has an individual strength ranking from 1 to 169.
techList: Built with Next.js, React, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS
All poker logic runs client-side — no server calls during gameplay
Hand evaluation uses combinatorial analysis of all C(7,5) = 21 five-card subsets
Available in 6 languages: English, Russian, French, German, Spanish, Italian

combinations.title: Poker & Combinations
combinations.pokerDesc1: Poker is a family of card games where players bet based on the strength of their five-card hand. The most popular variant is Texas Hold'em, where each player receives two hole cards and uses five community cards on the table to make the best five-card combination.
combinations.pokerDesc2: The winner is determined either by the best combination at showdown or when all opponents fold. Reading opponents, managing your bankroll, and making mathematically sound decisions are the keys to success.

hands.name: Royal Flush
hands.description: The strongest hand in poker. Five highest cards of the same suit: ace, king, queen, jack, and ten.

hands.name: Straight Flush
hands.description: Five consecutive cards of the same suit. For example, five through nine of hearts.

hands.name: Four of a Kind
hands.description: Four cards of the same rank. For example, four kings plus any fifth card.

hands.name: Full House
hands.description: Three cards of one rank and a pair of another. For example, three queens and two sevens.

hands.name: Flush
hands.description: Five cards of the same suit, not necessarily consecutive. The highest card determines the flush strength.

hands.name: Straight
hands.description: Five consecutive cards of different suits. An ace can be the highest or lowest card.

hands.name: Three of a Kind
hands.description: Three cards of the same rank and two unmatched cards. Also called a 'set' or 'trips'.

hands.name: Two Pair
hands.description: Two different pairs plus one card. Strength is determined by the higher pair, then the lower.

hands.name: One Pair
hands.description: Two cards of the same rank and three unmatched cards. The most common winning hand.

hands.name: High Card
hands.description: If no combination is made, the hand with the highest card wins. Cards are compared in descending order.

call.title: To Call or Not to Call
call.intro: You're facing a bet. Should you call or fold? Make your decision, then see the math behind it.
call.explanation: You need {potOdds}% equity to break even on a call. You have {equity}% equity.
call.practStrong: This is a very strong call. Your equity significantly exceeds the pot odds. In practice, you should call (or even raise) almost always.
call.practGood: This is a solid call. Your equity comfortably exceeds pot odds. Calling here is standard play.
call.practMarginal: This is a marginal spot. The math is very close to break-even. In practice, other factors matter: opponent tendencies, position, stack sizes, and implied odds. Experienced players might go either way.
call.practBad: The math says fold, but it's not a huge mistake to call. If you have strong implied odds or your opponent over-bluffs, a call could still be reasonable.
call.practTerrible: This is a clear fold. Your equity is well below what you need. Calling here consistently loses chips. Save your stack for better spots.

should3bet.title: Preflop Play
should3bet.intro: Your opponent opens with a raise preflop. You must decide: 3-bet for value, call to see a flop, or fold. Your position at the table changes the math — in position you can play wider, out of position you need a stronger hand.
should3bet.diff1: Very easy — obvious premium or trash hands
should3bet.diff2: Easy — clear-cut situations with standard opens
should3bet.diff3: Medium — borderline hands, requires equity estimation
should3bet.diff4: Hard — thin margins between call and 3-bet
should3bet.diff5: Expert — deceptive hand strength, position traps, razor-thin edges
should3bet.explanation: With {equity}% equity vs the open range: above {threeBet}% → 3-bet, {call}%–{threeBet}% → call, below {call}% → fold.
should3bet.practThreeBet: Your hand is strong enough to 3-bet for value. 3-betting isolates the opener, builds the pot with a range advantage, and denies equity to weaker hands. In position this is especially powerful — you control the action postflop. Out of position, 3-betting with premium hands compensates for the positional disadvantage by taking the initiative.
should3bet.practCall: Your hand has enough equity to continue but 3-betting would be marginal. In position, calling (flatting) is often preferred — you keep the pot manageable and leverage your positional advantage postflop. Out of position, calling is riskier since you'll act first on every street; consider whether the hand plays well multiway or has good implied odds.
should3bet.practFold: Your hand doesn't have sufficient equity against a typical open-raising range. Out of position, folding marginal hands is standard — playing weak hands OOP is one of the biggest leaks in poker. In position you can occasionally defend wider, but without specific reads on the opener, folding is the disciplined play here.

threebet.title: 3-Bet Situations
threebet.intro: You opened with a raise and your opponent 3-bets. Should you call or fold?
threebet.explanation: You need {potOdds}% equity to call profitably. Against a typical 3-bet range you have {equity}% equity.
threebet.practStrong: Your hand is very strong against the 3-bet range. Calling is clear — you might even 4-bet for value.
threebet.practGood: Solid call. Your hand has enough equity to continue. Suited connectors and strong broadways play well here.
threebet.practMarginal: Marginal spot. Consider: how tight is the opponent's 3-bet range? Do you have position? Suited/connected hands call better than raw high cards.
threebet.practBad: Leaning toward fold. If the opponent 3-bets wide, calling can work. Without reads, folding is safer.
threebet.practTerrible: Clear fold. Not enough equity or playability to continue against a 3-bet.
